query_text:
Sequential LLM decoding is slow because each token needs a full
large-model forward pass; how can a small draft model propose
tokens in parallel that the large model verifies without quality
loss?
paraphrase_essence: Making N interdependent decisions in strict sequence forces O(N) expensive evaluations of a large model, causing latency to scale with output length even when many decisions are easy or could be parallelized without hurting quality.

paraphrases.LLM_NLP: Autoregressive text generation with a large language model is slow because each next token requires a full, costly forward pass; latency grows with sequence length even when many continuations are predictable, constraining responsiveness under strict quality requirements.
paraphrases.MULTIMODAL: In vision-language generation and VQA, emitting text one token at a time with a heavy cross-modal model is slow; each word incurs expensive visual–textual computation, so answer latency grows linearly even when much of the continuation is visually obvious and quality cannot be reduced.
paraphrases.CV: High-resolution detection/segmentation that evaluates numerous regions or pixels with a heavy backbone in a sequential or exhaustive manner is slow; runtime is dominated by repeatedly applying the large model to many candidate locations even though most are easy negatives, under tight accuracy and latency budgets.
paraphrases.RL: Action selection that queries a large policy or planner at every timestep is slow because per-step evaluation accumulates over long horizons; serial decision-making dominates control latency even when stretches of the trajectory admit obvious actions, while performance must be preserved.
paraphrases.IR_REC: Relevance estimation that scores each candidate against a query using a heavy interaction model in sequence is slow; per-item expensive scoring dominates latency even though most candidates are easily dismissible, yet final ranking quality must not degrade.
paraphrases.SPEECH: Autoregressive ASR or TTS that emits tokens sequentially with a large acoustic–linguistic model is slow because each token requires a full pass; latency scales with utterance length even when long spans are predictable, limiting real-time operation under strict accuracy constraints.
paraphrases.ROBOTICS: Manipulation and motion planning pipelines that assess grasps or trajectories with high-fidelity models or checks one-by-one are slow; per-candidate expensive evaluation dominates cycle time even though many options are obviously infeasible, while safety and success rates must be maintained.

seeds.lens:
Sequential LLM decoding is slow because each token needs a full
large-model forward pass; how can a small draft model propose
tokens in parallel that the large model verifies without quality

relevance.reasoning: The seed's mechanism involves a small predictor (low-rank model) that anticipates which neurons will be active, enabling selective loading of weights — this is a form of cheap model predicting behavior of a larger model. The query requires a small draft model proposing tokens that a large model verifies (speculative decoding). While both involve a small model guiding computation of a larger model, the seed's mechanism is about weight loading/sparsity prediction for flash memory constraints, not token proposal and parallel verification. The underlying "small predictor guides large model computation" idea has thematic overlap, but the specific mechanism (neuron activation prediction for flash I/O reduction) does not transfer to the token drafting/verification pipeline without substantial redesign.

query_text:
VLM inference latency scales with visual token count but most
tokens are redundant; how can we identify and remove or merge
low-information visual tokens at inference without retraining
the encoder?
paraphrase_essence: Inference cost scales with the number of input elements even when many are redundant, and there is no inference-time mechanism to skip or compress them without retraining, causing unnecessary latency.

paraphrases.LLM_NLP: LLM inference is slow when long contexts force processing of every token, even though large spans are boilerplate or repetitive; with a frozen model, there is no way to drop or compress low-information tokens at inference, so latency grows with length.
paraphrases.MULTIMODAL: Vision-language inference is costly because latency scales with the count of visual tokens, many of which are background or duplicate content; with a fixed encoder, redundant visual tokens cannot be reduced at inference, so response time remains high.
paraphrases.CV: In visual perception, dense feature extraction is slow when high-resolution inputs yield many patch or pixel features despite broad uniform or uninformative regions; with a fixed extractor, inference cannot adapt the token budget to content, wasting compute on redundancy.
paraphrases.RL: Online control fails to meet real-time constraints when the policy must process high-dimensional observations containing many redundant visual features; with a frozen representation, each step incurs full-state computation, inflating action latency.
paraphrases.IR_REC: Retrieval and recommendation are slow when large candidate sets with many near-duplicates or low-signal items must all be scored; with fixed encoders, inference still evaluates every candidate, so latency scales with candidate count rather than information content.
paraphrases.SPEECH: Speech and audio models are slow when long utterances expand into many frames or time–frequency bins, even across silence or stationary noise; with a fixed frontend, inference pays for every acoustic token, degrading throughput despite redundancy.
paraphrases.ROBOTICS: Robot perception and planning are slow when dense sensor representations (point clouds, occupancy grids) include many empty or repetitive elements; with frozen perceptual modules, the stack processes all elements each cycle, increasing latency despite low incremental information.

query_text:
Inputs to generative models vary in complexity but standard
decoding allocates fixed compute per token; how can a model
adaptively allocate more or fewer computation steps without
degrading quality?
paraphrase_essence: Uniform per-decision compute ignores variable difficulty, wasting resources on easy cases and under-processing hard cases, which forces a speed-quality tradeoff under fixed budgets.

paraphrases.LLM_NLP: Sequential text generation spends a fixed amount of computation on every token regardless of uncertainty, wasting time on predictable continuations and under-processing ambiguous steps, which inflates latency or degrades output quality under a compute budget.
paraphrases.MULTIMODAL: Cross-modal generation applies a constant per-token or per-region compute budget even when visual-linguistic ambiguity varies widely, overprocessing clear content and underprocessing ambiguous spans, causing either slow inference or fidelity errors under tight limits.
paraphrases.CV: Per-image inference typically runs a uniform-depth computation across all pixels and regions, wasting cycles on easy background and large salient objects while failing on small, occluded, or low-contrast areas that need more processing under a fixed budget.
paraphrases.RL: Policies often use a constant decision-time per timestep across states of varying difficulty, squandering compute in trivial states while providing too little deliberation in high-uncertainty or high-stakes states, reducing returns under real-time constraints.
paraphrases.IR_REC: Retrieval and ranking pipelines devote a fixed scoring effort per query or candidate, spending as much time on obvious matches or rejects as on borderline cases, which wastes latency and under-examines ambiguous results within strict service-level budgets.
paraphrases.SPEECH: Speech decoders commonly allocate a fixed compute per frame or token regardless of acoustic or linguistic ambiguity, overprocessing silence and clean segments while giving noisy, coarticulated, or code-switched spans too little computation, increasing errors or delay.
paraphrases.ROBOTICS: Control loops and planners run with a fixed computation budget per cycle independent of scene complexity, wasting time in free-space motion while providing insufficient planning for contact-rich or cluttered interactions, leading to hesitations or failures under real-time limits.

query_text:
Long-context LLMs accumulate large KV caches that dominate
memory bandwidth; how can we compress or selectively evict
cache entries while preserving the model's ability to attend
to distant relevant tokens?
paraphrase_essence: Maintaining full, fine-grained history over long sequences creates a memory/bandwidth bottleneck, yet discarding or over-summarizing that history degrades the ability to use distant dependencies.

paraphrases.LLM_NLP: Long-context language models are bandwidth-bound because per-token history state grows with sequence length; naive dropping or heavy compression causes failures to use distant context that remains relevant.
paraphrases.MULTIMODAL: Cross-modal processing of long video–text streams is costly because storing and moving shared memory for many past frames and utterances dominates bandwidth; trimming older cues causes failures to ground current outputs on events far back in the stream.
paraphrases.CV: Long video understanding is memory-intensive because features from many prior frames must be retained; evicting early-frame features causes tracking and recognition to fail when long-range temporal cues are needed.
paraphrases.RL: Policies for long, partially observed tasks are bottlenecked by internal-state storage because episodes accumulate large histories; truncation causes state estimation and long-horizon credit assignment to fail.
paraphrases.IR_REC: Session-based retrieval and recommendation are expensive to serve because full interaction histories and long queries inflate per-request storage and bandwidth; cutting history leads to relevance failures when intent depends on early signals.
paraphrases.SPEECH: Streaming long-form speech and dialogue is bandwidth-heavy because detailed acoustic and linguistic context must be preserved over long durations; pruning past context causes recognition and speaker-tracking to fail when distant dependencies matter.
paraphrases.ROBOTICS: On-board autonomy for long-horizon manipulation or navigation is constrained by memory bandwidth because rich histories of observations and contacts accumulate; forgetting earlier events causes planning and control to fail when distant information is required.

query_text:
Low-bit quantization preserves perplexity but degrades emergent
abilities like in-context learning and chain-of-thought; how
can we quantize weights and activations while protecting the
components most responsible for these abilities?
paraphrase_essence: Uniform low-bit compression that preserves coarse average metrics can still cripple fragile higher-order behaviors because a small, precision-sensitive subset of parameters and activations disproportionately governs them under tight memory and latency budgets.

paraphrases.LLM_NLP: Low-bit quantization that matches perplexity degrades in-context learning, multi-step reasoning, and long-context recall because these behaviors depend on precision-sensitive pathways, and identifying which weights and activations cannot be coarsened under tight deployment budgets remains difficult.
paraphrases.MULTIMODAL: In vision–language models, compression that preserves captioning loss or retrieval scores degrades cross-modal reasoning, OCR-heavy queries, and referring expressions, indicating that alignment and fusion signals are precision-sensitive while it is unclear which components must retain higher fidelity within edge constraints.
paraphrases.CV: For visual perception, uniform low-bit quantization that maintains average classification accuracy degrades small-object detection, boundary quality in segmentation, and long-range context use, because a few feature paths are highly precision-sensitive and it is hard to determine what must remain high-precision under memory limits.
paraphrases.RL: In reinforcement learning, compressing policy and value networks to low precision can keep short-horizon returns stable yet degrade long-horizon credit assignment, sparse-reward success, and zero-shot transfer, as precision-sensitive state and update pathways are perturbed and it is unclear which parts cannot be coarsened on limited onboard compute.
paraphrases.IR_REC: In retrieval and ranking, low-bit models that preserve head-query offline metrics still mis-rank rare, multi-hop, or long-tail items, showing that subtle interaction signals are precision-sensitive while it is hard to pinpoint which scoring components must retain precision within serving latency and cost budgets.
paraphrases.SPEECH: In speech and audio models, uniform quantization that keeps WER or MOS near baseline degrades code-switching, speaker attribution, emotion/prosody cues, and long-form consistency, implying discourse and paralinguistic signals are precision-sensitive while the precision needs of specific time spans and components remain hard to identify under device constraints.
paraphrases.ROBOTICS: On-robot compression that leaves nominal tracking acceptable destabilizes dexterous manipulation, contact-rich transitions, and long-horizon task sequencing, because small precision losses in critical perception–control loops amplify downstream, and isolating which computations must remain high-precision under size, weight, and power limits is unresolved.

query_text:
Updating billions of parameters to adapt a pretrained LLM is
prohibitive on consumer hardware; how can we adapt the model
with a small number of additional parameters and match full
fine-tuning performance?
paraphrase_essence: Adapting very large pretrained models is infeasible on limited hardware because updating and storing changes to most parameters is required, while matching full fine-tuning quality with only a tiny parameter budget is needed but unavailable.

paraphrases.LLM_NLP: Full-model fine-tuning of pretrained language models is VRAM- and bandwidth-bound on commodity hardware because adapting each task requires updating and storing billions of weights, while an approach that matches full fine-tuning quality by modifying only a small parameter budget is needed but not available.
paraphrases.MULTIMODAL: Adapting large vision-language models to new image domains or instruction styles is impractical on low-resource setups because visual/text encoders and fusion components must be updated and saved per task, while parameter-efficient adaptation that preserves cross-modal grounding at full fine-tuning quality is needed but out of reach.
paraphrases.CV: Specializing high-capacity vision backbones for detection/segmentation/classification is slow and memory-heavy when each dataset requires end-to-end weight updates and separate checkpoints, while matching full fine-tuning accuracy with only minimal additional parameters is needed but not delivered.
paraphrases.RL: Policy transfer across tasks is compute- and sample-intensive when all network weights must be updated, so on-device or few-episode adaptation fails to meet performance targets; achieving full fine-tuning returns while changing only a small parameter set is needed but unavailable.
paraphrases.IR_REC: Domain or vertical adaptation of large ranking/retrieval models is costly in production because full towers must be fine-tuned and stored per use case, inflating latency and memory, while attaining full fine-tuning ranking quality with a tiny parameter budget is required but missing.
paraphrases.SPEECH: Personalizing ASR/TTS to speakers, accents, or environments is prohibitive on edge hardware when it entails updating and retaining changes to the entire model, while parameter-light adaptation that preserves full fine-tuning word error rate or naturalness is needed but not available.
paraphrases.ROBOTICS: On-robot adaptation of visuomotor policies to new objects, dynamics, or tools is blocked by the need to retrain all network weights, which is too slow and power-hungry, while achieving full fine-tuning control performance with only a small number of additional parameters is needed but currently unavailable.

prohibitive on consumer hardware; how can we adapt the model
with a small number of additional parameters and match full
fine-tuning performance?

relevance.reasoning: The seed's mechanism involves embedding smaller weight matrices into larger ones via linear transformations to share parameters across multiple model sizes, reducing total parameter count. The query requires adapting a large pretrained LLM with a small number of additional parameters (parameter-efficient fine-tuning). While both involve parameter efficiency, the seed's mechanism is about sharing parameters across multi-granularity models via masked matrix multiplication, not about adding a small adapter or low-rank update to a frozen pretrained model. The mechanism addresses a different problem (multi-size model training efficiency) and does not directly provide a lever for PEFT-style adaptation of frozen LLM weights.

query_text:
Vision-language models trained on web data degrade when images
come from a shifted distribution like medical or satellite;
how can we maintain cross-modal alignment under unseen domain
shift without target-domain labels?
paraphrase_essence: Alignment learned on one data distribution fails under unseen domain shift, and without target-domain labels preserving the necessary correspondence for accurate predictions is hard.

paraphrases.LLM_NLP: Language models and text encoders trained on general web corpora misinterpret specialized or out-of-distribution text (e.g., clinical or legal), and with no in-domain annotations their semantic consistency and calibration degrade under shift.
paraphrases.MULTIMODAL: Vision-language systems grounded on web image–text pairs lose cross-modal alignment when images come from unseen domains like medical or satellite, and in the absence of target-domain pairs their grounding drifts and zero-shot performance collapses.
paraphrases.CV: Visual classifiers, detectors, and segmenters trained on natural images misclassify or miss structures in medical or aerial imagery, and without target-domain labels their class-conditional boundaries remain misaligned to new appearance statistics.
paraphrases.RL: Goal-conditioned policies that rely on visual observations or language goals fail to achieve tasks when the observation distribution shifts (e.g., textures, sensors, lighting), and with no rewards or demonstrations in the target domain they cannot realign behavior to new perceptual cues.
paraphrases.IR_REC: Retrieval and ranking models tuned on general web queries and clicks mis-rank documents in specialized corpora due to distribution shift, and lacking in-domain relevance judgments they cannot maintain stable query–document semantic alignment.
paraphrases.SPEECH: ASR and speech–text models trained on broad accents and topics break on domain-specific acoustics and vocabulary, and without target-domain transcriptions their audio–text alignment degrades, inflating error rates.
paraphrases.ROBOTICS: Embodied agents conditioned on vision and language that work in lab settings fail to ground instructions and perceive affordances in new sites or sensor configurations, and without on-site labels or demonstrations the perception–language–action correspondence drifts and task success drops.

query_text:
Object detectors fail on rare classes with few or no labeled
instances; how can we detect and localize long-tail or novel
objects without per-class supervision?
paraphrase_essence: Systems that require per-class supervision fail on long-tail or novel categories; detecting and localizing rare items is unreliable when labeled instances are scarce or absent.

paraphrases.LLM_NLP: Text classifiers and sequence taggers that rely on per-label training data underperform on long-tail or unseen categories; span identification and label assignment fail when entity types or intents have few or no labeled examples.
paraphrases.MULTIMODAL: Vision-language grounding struggles to detect and localize references to rare or novel objects in images or video when paired category annotations are sparse or unavailable, leading to missed or incorrect region grounding.
paraphrases.CV: Object detectors optimized with per-class bounding-box labels miss or mislocalize tail and unseen categories because supervision is too sparse or absent to learn reliable detectors.
paraphrases.RL: Policies trained with task-specific rewards or demonstrations fail to recognize and target rare or unseen goal objects or events; identification and localization of novel task-relevant elements in observations break down when per-goal supervision is missing.
paraphrases.IR_REC: Retrieval and recommendation systems that depend on per-item interactions or relevance judgments perform poorly on long-tail or cold-start items and rare queries, failing to surface or highlight relevant content for novel topics with no feedback.
paraphrases.SPEECH: ASR, keyword spotting, and acoustic event detection that rely on per-class supervision degrade on rare names, novel words, dialects, or events, and cannot reliably detect or temporally localize them when labeled audio is scarce or missing.
paraphrases.ROBOTICS: Perception and manipulation pipelines that assume per-object models or labeled affordances fail to detect, localize, and handle long-tail or novel objects, making grasping and placement unreliable when object-level supervision is unavailable.

query_text:
Online RL is expensive but offline data suffers from
distribution mismatch between behavior and target policy;
how can we learn a strong policy from logs alone while
avoiding extrapolation errors?
paraphrase_essence: Learning a high-performing decision policy solely from static logs generated by a different behavior is unreliable: off-support actions incur biased value estimates and extrapolation errors, yet collecting on-policy data is costly.

paraphrases.LLM_NLP: Learning a dialogue or instruction-following policy purely from static conversation logs is unreliable because prompts and responses outside the logged distribution receive biased credit and trigger degeneration, while interactive feedback collection is costly.
paraphrases.MULTIMODAL: Training a vision–language assistant from frozen image–text interaction logs is brittle under new visual contexts and user intents: out-of-support question–region combinations are mis-scored, yet collecting on-policy multimodal interactions is expensive.
paraphrases.CV: Perception models trained on curated datasets reflect a biased sampling of scenes and object contexts; predictions in sparsely supported regions of feature space become overconfident and wrong, while acquiring labeled data matching the target deployment distribution is expensive.
paraphrases.RL: Offline policy learning from behavior logs is brittle: value estimates for actions not well covered by the dataset are biased, causing extrapolation errors and performance collapse, while online data collection and exploration are costly.
paraphrases.IR_REC: Training a recommender from historical exposure and click logs is biased: counterfactual estimates for rarely shown items and slates are unreliable, leading off-policy rankers to overfit unsupported actions, while online A/B testing is costly and risky.
paraphrases.SPEECH: Learning dialog management or turn-taking policies for voice assistants from past audio–action logs is unreliable because the logs cover a narrow distribution of contexts and actions; off-support decisions receive misleading value estimates, while collecting interactive on-device data is expensive.
paraphrases.ROBOTICS: Offline robot policy learning from teleoperation or demonstration logs fails to generalize safely: state–action pairs outside the dataset receive over-optimistic value estimates, causing compounding errors at deployment, while gathering on-policy experience on hardware is slow, risky, and costly.

query_text:
Dense retrieval surfaces topically relevant documents but
ranks them poorly when relevance depends on subtle context;
how can we rerank candidates using contextual signals
without re-encoding the entire corpus?
paraphrase_essence: Context-agnostic representations retrieve broadly relevant candidates but misrank those whose relevance depends on fine-grained, query-specific cues, and obtaining accurate context-conditioned scores at scale is prohibitive if it requires reprocessing the entire corpus per request.

paraphrases.LLM_NLP: Context-agnostic passage retrieval surfaces on-topic texts but orders them poorly when discourse, pragmatics, or conversation history determine relevance; computing context-conditioned scores for many passages is too slow because it would require reprocessing texts with the full context for each query. How can accurate context-sensitive passage ranking be obtained without re-encoding the corpus per request?
paraphrases.MULTIMODAL: Fixed image/text encodings return broadly matching items but rank them poorly when fine-grained relations, spatial/temporal cues, or dialog context drive relevance; scoring the gallery conditioned on the current text is costly because it implies recomputing features for all candidates. How can cross-modal retrieval capture context without recomputing the entire index each time?
paraphrases.CV: Global image descriptors retrieve visually similar images but misrank results when subtle attributes or scene context define similarity; applying a context-aware scorer across the whole database is slow because it would require reprocessing every image per query. How can image retrieval achieve fine-grained, context-conditioned ranking under these constraints?
paraphrases.RL: Action selection based on coarse state features surfaces plausible actions but misranks them when nuanced task context or history matters; accurately evaluating all options with detailed, context-conditioned rollouts is too costly for real-time decision-making. How can policies obtain context-sensitive action rankings without exhaustive recomputation across the action set?
paraphrases.IR_REC: Dense retrieval returns semantically related documents but orders them poorly when nuanced intent or session context determines relevance; computing context-conditioned scores for many candidates is costly because reprocessing the full corpus per request is infeasible. How can better context-aware ranking be achieved without re-encoding the entire corpus?
paraphrases.SPEECH: Embedding-based spoken content retrieval surfaces acoustically similar segments but misranks them when lexical meaning or disambiguation depends on surrounding discourse or speaker context; rescoring the full archive with context-conditioned models is expensive because it would require re-encoding long audio per query. How can speech retrieval or recognition produce context-sensitive rankings without reprocessing all audio?
paraphrases.ROBOTICS: Precomputed grasp or trajectory candidates identify generally feasible options but rank them poorly when task- and scene-specific constraints (e.g., fragility, occlusion, downstream goals) determine success; evaluating every candidate with detailed, context-aware perception and physics is too slow for real-time control. How can robots prioritize candidates using current context without exhaustively re-evaluating the entire set each cycle?

query_text:
Speech recognition for low-resource languages or specialized
domains lacks transcribed audio; how can we leverage synthetic
data or transfer learning to reach competitive accuracy with
minimal real labeled data?
paraphrase_essence: Target-domain performance collapses when supervised learning depends on abundant in-domain labels that are scarce or costly, and knowledge learned elsewhere does not generalize under distribution shift.

paraphrases.LLM_NLP: Language understanding and generation for low-resource languages or narrow domains is inaccurate because high-quality, in-domain annotations and parallel corpora are scarce, and models trained on other languages or general domains mis-handle morphology, vocabulary, and discourse under domain shift.
paraphrases.MULTIMODAL: Cross-modal tasks targeting low-resource languages or niche domains are brittle because paired image–text supervision in the target setting is scarce, causing misalignment and degraded grounding when trained primarily on pairs from other languages or domains.
paraphrases.CV: Visual classification, detection, and segmentation in specialized domains lag in accuracy because dense, expert annotations are limited and expensive, and models developed on generic imagery fail under domain shift and rare-class scarcity.
paraphrases.RL: Policy learning in new tasks and environments is sample-inefficient and unstable when task-specific experience or reward-rich signals are scarce, and behaviors learned in different dynamics or observation regimes fail to generalize, making data collection costly.
paraphrases.IR_REC: Search and recommendation in a new locale, language, or catalog suffers cold-start errors because relevance judgments and interaction logs are sparse, and ranking models tuned on other domains mis-rank due to vocabulary, item, and intent mismatch while obtaining high-quality labels is expensive.
paraphrases.SPEECH: Automatic speech recognition for low-resource languages or specialized acoustic domains underperforms because transcribed audio is unavailable or too costly to obtain at scale, and models trained on high-resource speech misrecognize phonetic inventories, prosody, and domain-specific lexicons under distribution shift.
paraphrases.ROBOTICS: Embodied manipulation or navigation for novel objects and settings is unreliable because task-labeled demonstrations and on-robot trial data are limited and costly to acquire, and skills developed in other labs or simulators break under morphology, sensor, and contact dynamics shifts.

query_text:
Robot policies trained in simulation often fail on real
hardware due to physics, sensor, and dynamics mismatch; how
can we transfer visuomotor policies sim-to-real with minimal
real-world demonstrations?
paraphrase_essence: Models trained in a simplified or proxy domain fail under deployment-domain shift because key dynamics and observations are mismatched, while acquiring sufficient target-domain supervision to adapt is costly.

paraphrases.LLM_NLP: Language models trained on static or synthetic corpora underperform in live, noisy, safety- and tool-constrained interactions due to deployment distribution shift, while high-quality human demonstrations and feedback in the target setting are scarce and expensive.
paraphrases.MULTIMODAL: Cross-modal models fit on clean web captions or rendered scenes struggle to ground language in real sensor streams because visual and acoustic statistics shift across capture conditions, and paired target-domain supervision is limited.
paraphrases.CV: Visual classifiers and detectors trained on synthetic or controlled images misclassify and miss objects in the wild due to texture, lighting, and sensor mismatch, and dense annotations for the target conditions are costly to obtain.
paraphrases.RL: Policies optimized in simulators overfit to modeled transitions and rewards and degrade under real-world delays, noise, and unmodeled dynamics, while safe online adaptation is constrained by limited, expensive trials.
paraphrases.IR_REC: Retrieval and ranking models tuned offline on historical or simulated logs generalize poorly in production because user intent, inventory, and feedback bias differ from training, and collecting sufficient unbiased online interactions is costly and risky.
paraphrases.SPEECH: Speech models trained on studio or simulated acoustics degrade on real microphones, accents, and far-field noise due to channel and prosody mismatch, while transcribed target-domain audio is scarce and expensive.
paraphrases.ROBOTICS: Visuomotor policies trained in simulation fail on real hardware because physics, sensing, and dynamics are mismatched, and obtaining enough real-world demonstrations to adapt them is time-consuming and costly.

query_text:
Models reason over compositional task structure but learned
representations are continuous and entangled; how can we
induce discrete, reusable components that support
compositional reasoning over novel combinations?
paraphrase_essence: When tasks are compositional, entangled continuous representations do not expose discrete, reusable parts, so models struggle to systematically recombine known elements to handle novel combinations.

paraphrases.LLM_NLP: Language models with dense, entangled representations blur primitives like entities, operators, and steps, so they fail to bind and recombine subskills for novel instruction sequences or compositional queries.
paraphrases.MULTIMODAL: Vision-language models use joint embeddings that conflate attributes, relations, and linguistic operators, causing failures on multi-hop grounding and unseen attribute–object pairings because components are not discretely reusable.
paraphrases.CV: Visual perception models learn holistic features that entangle parts, attributes, and spatial relations, leading to binding errors and poor generalization to unseen part–whole or attribute–object compositions (e.g., color–shape swaps).
paraphrases.RL: End-to-end policies encode behaviors in entangled latents, making it hard to isolate and reuse atomic behaviors, so agents underperform on tasks formed by new sequences, combinations, or constraints of known subtasks.
paraphrases.IR_REC: Embedding-based retrieval and recommendation conflate multiple intent facets in continuous vectors, so systems struggle on multi-constraint or compositional queries and fail to generalize to unseen attribute combinations.
paraphrases.SPEECH: Speech and audio models represent content, speaker, and prosody in entangled spaces, so they misbind or cannot recombine these factors for novel word formations, speaker–style mixes, or compositional acoustic events.
paraphrases.ROBOTICS: Manipulation and navigation policies are learned as monolithic, entangled controllers, preventing decomposition into reusable motion or interaction primitives, so robots cannot reliably compose known behaviors to execute novel task combinations or reordered subgoals.

query_text:
Complex sequential tasks have nested goal structure but flat
policies struggle with credit assignment over long horizons;
how can we learn or impose a hierarchy that decomposes the
task and accelerates learning?
paraphrase_essence: When tasks contain nested subgoals and long temporal dependencies, flat decision-making provides weak credit assignment over long horizons, making learning slow and sample-inefficient.

paraphrases.LLM_NLP: Autoregressive next-token objectives give only local feedback, so credit assignment across many decoding steps within a long context length is weak; dependencies that require coordinated attention heads persist and perplexity gains fail to yield reliable long-form control.
paraphrases.MULTIMODAL: In VQA and captioning with multi-step reasoning, supervision arrives only at the final text output and query-conditioned attention over numerous visual tokens disperses gradients; cross-modal alignment of intermediate concepts is brittle and VQA accuracy drops on compositional queries.
paraphrases.CV: Dense predictors trained on high-resolution feature maps with large receptive fields receive weak supervision for part–whole structure; early anchor box selection errors dominate and downstream segmentation masks for small nested objects degrade at high spatial resolution.
paraphrases.RL: With sparse reward signals and long rollouts, a single flat policy and value function provide weak credit assignment over temporally extended subgoals, leading to poor sample efficiency and unstable learning.
paraphrases.IR_REC: In multi-hop retrieval and conversational search, independent query-document scoring during reranking offers no feedback for early hops; errors in candidate pool pruning cascade, recall@k degrades, and final-step cross-encoder signals cannot attribute credit upstream.
paraphrases.SPEECH: For long-form ASR, sequence-level learning over thousands of acoustic frames yields diffuse gradients; phoneme alignment drifts over time, WER increases on extended utterances, and streaming latency constraints further obscure attribution to early frames.
paraphrases.ROBOTICS: In long-horizon manipulation within the sensorimotor loop, a monolithic manipulation policy receives sparse rewards; errors in contact reasoning early in the motion plan are not properly credited, inaccuracies in the dynamics model accumulate, and data efficiency is poor.

query_text:
Generating intermediate reasoning steps before the final
answer sharply improves LLM accuracy; how can we apply
analogous intermediate-computation patterns to vision,
reinforcement learning, or recommendation models?
paraphrase_essence: Direct-to-final prediction without explicit intermediate computation is brittle on compositional or long-horizon tasks because it lacks latent states to decompose, verify, and allocate compute across subproblems.

paraphrases.LLM_NLP: Final-answer-only decoding compresses multi-step queries into a few decoding steps, and accuracy degrades because attention heads lack persistent token states in the KV cache to track subgoals, yielding elevated perplexity on compositional prompts.
paraphrases.MULTIMODAL: Predicting a caption or VQA answer directly from a global image embedding fails on compositional queries because query-conditioned attention lacks intermediate cross-modal alignment over visual tokens to ground entities and relations, reducing VQA accuracy.
paraphrases.CV: Direct label prediction from a single feature map without intermediate instance grouping fails in cluttered scenes because limited receptive field interactions and missing anchor box–level states prevent consistent aggregation, degrading segmentation mask quality.
paraphrases.RL: A purely reactive policy that maps observations to final actions fails on long-horizon tasks with sparse reward signals because no intermediate trajectory structure exposes subgoals for the value function, causing poor credit assignment and low sample efficiency.
paraphrases.IR_REC: Single-stage retrieval that returns a final ranked list directly from an embedding index underperforms on multi-facet queries because no intermediate evidence accumulation over the candidate pool refines query-document scores, hurting recall@k.
paraphrases.SPEECH: End-to-end decoding that emits final words without intermediate phoneme constraints fails in noisy or long-form audio because acoustic-frame to grapheme mapping lacks stable alignment, increasing WER.
paraphrases.ROBOTICS: A manipulation policy that selects a final goal pose without intermediate waypoints fails in contact-rich tasks because the sensorimotor loop lacks a motion plan with explicit contact reasoning under the dynamics model, leading to frequent task failures.

query_text:
Models attending to long contexts (videos, long documents,
episodes) suffer from quadratic attention cost and forgetting;
how can we maintain effective memory of distant information
without exhausting compute?
paraphrase_essence: When inputs span long horizons, maintaining useful access to far-past information requires interactions that scale superlinearly (often quadratically), exhausting compute/memory and inducing recency-biased forgetting under fixed budgets.

paraphrases.LLM_NLP: In language models, full self-attention over tokens scales quadratically with context length, KV cache memory grows with sequence size, and attention heads underweight early content, causing distant in-context examples to be ignored under practical compute/memory budgets.
paraphrases.MULTIMODAL: In video–language models, query-conditioned attention over all visual tokens in long clips scales quadratically and saturates memory on image embeddings, degrading cross-modal alignment to temporally distant frames and reducing VQA accuracy on long-horizon queries.
paraphrases.CV: In dense vision, global self-attention across patch tokens grows quadratically with spatial resolution, forcing truncation or diluted interactions so distant regions on the feature map fail to inform each other, yielding inconsistent segmentation masks in large scenes.
paraphrases.RL: In reinforcement learning, a policy operating over long rollout horizons faces prohibitive computation to retain dependencies across the trajectory, and weak credit assignment causes distant reward signals to be ignored under limited memory and time budgets.
paraphrases.IR_REC: In retrieval and reranking, cross-encoder query–document scoring on long documents incurs near-quadratic attention cost, so distal evidence is truncated or overlooked and recall@k drops even when the candidate pool contains relevant passages.
paraphrases.SPEECH: In attention-based ASR, decoder–encoder attention over thousands of acoustic frames is memory- and compute-heavy, and alignment drifts so distant phonemes are under-attended, increasing WER under streaming latency constraints.
paraphrases.ROBOTICS: In long-horizon manipulation, preserving task-relevant state across the sensorimotor loop is costly for the manipulation policy, and under tight control-cycle budgets distant contact events are forgotten, producing motion plans that miss early cues.

seeds.lens:
Models attending to long contexts (videos, long documents,
episodes) suffer from quadratic attention cost and forgetting;
how can we maintain effective memory of distant information
without exhausting compute?

relevance.reasoning: The query asks about mechanisms for maintaining effective memory of distant information in long-context settings (videos, documents, episodes) while managing quadratic attention cost. The seed's mechanism involves visual prompt learning with uncertainty-guided EMA updates to handle continual domain shifts and catastrophic forgetting in a cloud-to-device transfer setting. While the seed does address "forgetting" in a continual learning sense, its mechanism (pixel-level visual prompts + uncertainty-weighted EMA + adversarial alignment) is designed for distribution shift adaptation, not for efficient attention over long sequences or memory of distant tokens. The forgetting problem in the seed is about model weights forgetting old domains, not about attention mechanisms failing to retain distant context. There is no transferable mechanism for long-context memory or quadratic attention cost reduction.

query_text:
Dense models compute every parameter for every input, wasting
compute when only a fraction is needed; how can we conditionally
activate only the relevant subnetwork at inference?
paraphrase_essence: Inference applies uniform dense computation across all parameters for every input, making cost scale with total model size even though only an input-specific subset is relevant.

paraphrases.LLM_NLP: Autoregressive generation is compute-inefficient because each decoding step evaluates all layers and attention heads over the full context length, so per-token latency scales with total parameter count rather than the small subset of activations that influence the next token, even with a KV cache.
paraphrases.MULTIMODAL: In vision–language models, cross-modal alignment is costly because query-conditioned attention attends to every visual token and image embedding at each text step, even when VQA accuracy depends on a small region, making throughput bound by dense computation.
paraphrases.CV: Dense visual perception is slow because high spatial resolution feature maps propagate all patch tokens through deep backbones and heads, although only a subset within the effective receptive field is needed to localize foreground, inflating latency for segmentation mask inference.
paraphrases.RL: Online control becomes compute-bound when the policy and value function are evaluated densely at every rollout step over the full observation and action dimensions, even though the reward signal along the trajectory depends on a small subset of features, causing latency that limits control frequency.
paraphrases.IR_REC: Neural reranking is costly because a cross-encoder computes a query–document score for every item in the candidate pool, so latency and throughput scale with pool size despite recall@k being determined by only a few high-quality candidates.
paraphrases.SPEECH: Streaming ASR incurs unnecessary compute because each acoustic frame drives the full spectrogram encoder–decoder regardless of silence or stable phoneme regions, raising streaming latency while WER constraints restrict aggressive pruning.
paraphrases.ROBOTICS: In the sensorimotor loop, real-time execution is slow when a manipulation policy and dynamics model are evaluated over the full state at each cycle and motion plan update, even though contact reasoning indicates only a limited set of active joints and contacts is relevant, causing deadlines to be missed.

query_text:
Large generalist models are accurate but expensive; smaller
specialists are cheap but worse on the specialist's own
domain; how can we transfer the relevant generalist capability
to a small specialist?
paraphrase_essence: When model capacity and compute are constrained, a domain-specific specialist cannot access the subset of generalist knowledge needed for its own domain, causing a persistent in-domain performance gap.

paraphrases.LLM_NLP: Small domain-specialized LLMs with limited attention heads and context length show elevated perplexity and brittle decoding on in-domain prompts because generalist priors over long-range token dependencies are unavailable under the KV-cache and parameter budget.
paraphrases.MULTIMODAL: With fewer visual tokens and lower-dimensional image embeddings, small domain-focused multimodal models exhibit degraded cross-modal alignment and reduced VQA accuracy on in-domain queries because the generalist model’s query-conditioned attention patterns are not retained under the capacity and latency budget.
paraphrases.CV: Compact, domain-specific vision encoders operating at reduced spatial resolution and receptive field produce lower-quality segmentation masks on in-domain images because high-level shape and texture priors are not representable within the constrained feature map capacity.
paraphrases.RL: Low-capacity, task-specific policies attain lower returns and require longer rollouts on their target tasks because state abstractions encoded in a high-capacity generalist policy/value function are unavailable, reducing sample efficiency under the same reward signal.
paraphrases.IR_REC: Under tight latency, a small in-domain ranker yields lower recall@k because interactions captured by a generalist cross-encoder are not preserved within a lightweight embedding index, degrading reranking quality over the candidate pool.
paraphrases.SPEECH: On-device, domain-specific ASR with constrained acoustic-frame context and model size exhibits higher WER on in-domain utterances because robust phoneme inventories and coarticulation alignment learned by a generalist system are unavailable under streaming-latency and memory limits.
paraphrases.ROBOTICS: Resource-limited, task-specific manipulation policies running in a tight sensorimotor loop achieve lower task success and unstable contact reasoning on their target skills because generalist priors over dynamics are unavailable under onboard compute and latency constraints.

query_text:
Test-time distributions diverge from training data along axes
the model never saw; how can we make models robust to such
shifts without observing the target distribution at training?
paraphrase_essence: Models optimized on a source distribution generalize poorly when test-time data shifts along unobserved factors, and robustness cannot be calibrated because target-domain samples are unavailable during training.

paraphrases.LLM_NLP: Language models trained on source corpora exhibit elevated perplexity and brittle next-token predictions when deployment prompts shift in token distribution or exceed seen context length, yet robustness cannot be calibrated because target-style in-context examples are unavailable during training.
paraphrases.MULTIMODAL: Vision–language models show degraded cross-modal alignment when test images and queries depart from training coverage (e.g., novel visual tokens, shifted image embeddings, or unseen phrasings), causing VQA accuracy to drop while no target-domain pairs exist during training to expose the miscalibration of query-conditioned attention.
paraphrases.CV: Detection and segmentation pipelines trained on a source dataset fail when test-time feature map statistics shift due to different spatial resolution, texture, or noise; anchor box priors and resulting segmentation masks become miscalibrated, with no target-domain frames available during training to quantify the drift.
paraphrases.RL: A policy and value function optimized under source dynamics generalize poorly when deployment dynamics or the reward signal shift, producing brittle trajectories and unstable rollouts while no target-environment experience is available during training.
paraphrases.IR_REC: Retrieval systems trained on head-query distributions suffer drops in recall@k when live traffic shifts in intent, vocabulary, or document domain; embedding index neighborhoods and query-document scores become misaligned across the candidate pool, and no target-query logs are available during training.
paraphrases.SPEECH: ASR models trained on a source corpus see increased WER when test-time spectrograms reflect unseen microphones, accents, or noise conditions; acoustic frame–level phoneme alignment drifts, and no target-domain audio is available during training.
paraphrases.ROBOTICS: A manipulation policy tuned in simulation degrades under sim-to-real shift when real-world contact reasoning and dynamics model mismatch occur, destabilizing the sensorimotor loop with no target-environment trials available during training.

query_text:
Spurious correlations make models rely on shortcuts that fail
under intervention; how can we generate counterfactual examples
that break these shortcuts and induce invariance to non-causal
features?
paraphrase_essence: Models trained on observational data overfit spurious correlations, causing predictions to fail under interventions or distribution shifts because they rely on non-causal features instead of causal signals.

paraphrases.LLM_NLP: Language models trained on observational corpora latch onto token co-occurrence and prompt-format heuristics; under negation flips, entity swaps, or perturbed in-context examples, predictions depend on non-causal surface cues, with attention heads ignoring causal tokens across decoding steps and accuracy degrading at similar perplexity.
paraphrases.MULTIMODAL: Vision–language models exhibit weak cross-modal alignment and exploit language priors, so when visual tokens and question phrases are decoupled via image–text mismatches or attribute swaps, query-conditioned attention collapses to text-only shortcuts and VQA accuracy drops despite unchanged image embeddings.
paraphrases.CV: Image classifiers and detectors learn context–label shortcuts in the feature map, with patch tokens and limited receptive fields emphasizing background textures; under object–context recomposition or style shifts, anchor box localization and segmentation masks fail as predictions hinge on non-causal cues.
paraphrases.RL: Policies trained from correlated rollouts and sparse reward signal overfit to observation confounders; when environment features are intervened on, the value function and action selection track spurious state variables, causing return collapse on shifted trajectories.
paraphrases.IR_REC: Learning-to-rank systems trained on click logs internalize position and presentation biases, so when snippets or URL tokens are perturbed, query–document scoring relies on these non-causal cues, reducing recall@k from the embedding index and degrading cross-encoder reranking in the candidate pool.
paraphrases.SPEECH: ASR models learn shortcuts from spectrogram patterns tied to channel and speaker artifacts; when acoustic frames are altered via codec shifts or phoneme-level edits, alignment between acoustics and transcript drifts, inflating WER even though linguistic content is unchanged.
paraphrases.ROBOTICS: End-to-end manipulation policy learning in the sensorimotor loop overfits scene-context correlations; when textures, lighting, or distractors are intervened on, the dynamics model and contact reasoning attend to non-causal cues, yielding behaviors that fail to transfer in sim-to-real settings.

query_text:
Deep models are overconfident even when wrong, making
downstream decisions unreliable; how can we calibrate
predicted probabilities to reflect actual likelihood,
especially under distribution shift?
paraphrase_essence: Predicted probabilities are miscalibrated—systematically overconfident and misaligned with true outcome frequencies—especially under distribution shift, undermining the reliability of downstream decisions.

paraphrases.LLM_NLP: In autoregressive language models, token probabilities derived from the logit scale are overconfident relative to empirical next-token accuracy, particularly under out-of-distribution prompts and atypical in-context examples, making perplexity and selective decoding thresholds unreliable across decoding steps.
paraphrases.MULTIMODAL: In vision–language models, predicted answer likelihoods conditioned on image embeddings and visual tokens are miscalibrated with respect to realized VQA accuracy, and this gap widens when cross-modal alignment drifts, undermining the reliability of query-conditioned attention scores for selective prediction.
paraphrases.CV: For detection and segmentation, class logits and objectness scores attached to anchor boxes on high-resolution feature maps are overconfident with respect to final IoU or mask quality, and the mismatch worsens under shifts in scale or illumination, making score thresholds and NMS decisions unreliable.
paraphrases.RL: In reinforcement learning, policy action probabilities and value function estimates are overconfident relative to returns observed in rollouts, particularly under off-policy trajectories or non-stationary reward signals, yielding unreliable risk-sensitive control.
paraphrases.IR_REC: In retrieval and ranking, query–document scores from a bi-encoder or cross-encoder are overconfident with respect to true relevance and click-through, and this miscalibration increases when the candidate pool distribution shifts, degrading thresholded filtering, reranking cutoffs, and recall@k targets.
paraphrases.SPEECH: In automatic speech recognition, phoneme posteriors computed over acoustic frames from the spectrogram are overconfident relative to empirical alignment and WER, especially under noise, accent, or channel shift, making beam pruning and endpointing thresholds unreliable.
paraphrases.ROBOTICS: In embodied control, uncertainty estimates within the sensorimotor loop—e.g., from the dynamics model or perception stack—are overconfident relative to actual task success, especially under sim-to-real gaps and unmodeled contact reasoning, causing manipulation policy safety margins to be unreliable.

unimodal data is abundant; how can we learn cross-modal
representations via contrastive objectives that exploit the
abundant unimodal data?
paraphrase_essence: Learning joint representations across modalities depends on scarce paired supervision, leaving abundant unimodal data underutilized and yielding weak cross-modal alignment and degraded downstream performance.

paraphrases.LLM_NLP: Cross-lingual representation learning is brittle when parallel sentence pairs are scarce: token-level alignment across languages relies on sequence co-occurrence, while vast monolingual corpora are ignored, producing poorly aligned embeddings, unstable attention heads under code-switching, and elevated perplexity for mixed-language context windows.
paraphrases.MULTIMODAL: Image–text pretraining stalls in domains with few captions: cross-modal alignment between visual tokens and text embeddings hinges on image–caption co-occurrence, yet large stores of unpaired images and domain text remain unused, depressing zero-shot retrieval and VQA accuracy.
paraphrases.CV: Supervised dense perception is limited when segmentation masks and bounding boxes are scarce: high-resolution feature maps require pixel-level or anchor-box supervision, while abundant unlabeled frames are ignored, yielding brittle receptive fields and degraded segmentation quality under distribution shift.
paraphrases.RL: Language-conditioned control underperforms when instruction–trajectory pairs are scarce: mapping goal text to a policy over actions depends on reward signals tied to aligned demonstrations, while extensive unlabeled rollouts provide no grounding, reducing sample efficiency and destabilizing value function estimates.
paraphrases.IR_REC: Retrieval training is constrained when query–document relevance pairs are sparse: accurate reranking and calibrated query–document scores depend on supervised clicks, while massive unpaired corpora and query logs remain unused, lowering recall@k and degrading the semantic structure of the embedding index for the candidate pool.
paraphrases.SPEECH: ASR–TTS representation learning degrades in low-resource settings where transcribed speech is scarce: robust acoustic–linguistic modeling needs phoneme alignment across acoustic frames and transcripts, yet large reserves of unpaired spectrograms or text are unused, inflating WER and weakening robustness to domain shifts.
paraphrases.ROBOTICS: Language-conditioned manipulation falters when instruction–trajectory datasets are scarce: the sensorimotor loop lacks sufficient paired supervision to ground a manipulation policy in natural language goals, while extensive unpaired robot logs and task manuals remain unexploited, impairing motion plan selection and contact reasoning in sim-to-real transfer.

query_text:
Masked-prediction pretraining transformed language and vision;
how can we adapt the same principle to other data types like
graphs, time series, or sensor streams, and what objective
design choices matter?
paraphrase_essence: Masked-prediction self-supervision transfers poorly across modalities when the corruption scheme and prediction target are misaligned with the modality’s structure and conditional dependencies, yielding weak downstream representations.

paraphrases.LLM_NLP: Masked language modeling degrades for downstream causal use when the token masking distribution and span length misalign with long-range discourse, causing attention heads to learn artificial cloze patterns and raising perplexity at large context length.
paraphrases.MULTIMODAL: Cross-modal pretraining fails to preserve grounding when masked visual token reconstruction breaks cross-modal alignment, leading query-conditioned attention to attend to spurious regions and reducing VQA accuracy and caption consistency.
paraphrases.CV: Pixel-level reconstruction objectives are misaligned with recognition when patch token masking overemphasizes textures, yielding feature maps with limited effective receptive field and degrading segmentation mask quality on object-centric tasks.
paraphrases.RL: Self-supervised trajectory modeling underperforms for control when state/action masking ignores the reward signal and long rollout horizon, producing representations that do not support reliable policy and value function estimation.
paraphrases.IR_REC: Pretraining that reconstructs masked terms in isolation is misaligned with retrieval when it does not calibrate the query-document score, yielding weak embedding indexes and lower recall@k during reranking of the candidate pool.
paraphrases.SPEECH: Frame-level masking harms ASR transfer when masks cut across phoneme boundaries and ignore alignment and streaming latency constraints, increasing WER and reducing the utility of learned acoustic representations.
paraphrases.ROBOTICS: Masked prediction over sensor streams is ineffective for visuomotor control when the corruption scheme ignores action-conditional dynamics in the sensorimotor loop, yielding features that do not support manipulation policy learning, contact reasoning, or robust sim-to-real transfer.

query_text:
Pretraining at scale is expensive; can we replace some real
data with synthetic curricula that exercise specific
capabilities, and how do we ensure synthetic data improves
rather than degrades downstream performance?
paraphrase_essence: When high-fidelity training data is costly, substituting with cheaper synthetic samples introduces distribution shift and coverage gaps that can cause negative transfer, and there is no reliable utility signal to ensure downstream performance does not degrade.

paraphrases.LLM_NLP: LLM pretraining at scale is costly, and substituting real text with synthetic token sequences often skews the token distribution and degrades behavior at large context length, so perplexity on the mixed corpus ceases to be a reliable proxy for downstream accuracy from in-context examples.
paraphrases.MULTIMODAL: Vision–language pretraining on billions of image–text pairs is expensive, and replacing real pairs with synthetic captions or rendered scenes disrupts cross-modal alignment between image embeddings and text, reducing VQA accuracy even when the pretraining loss over visual tokens is low.
paraphrases.CV: For dense perception, large annotated corpora are costly, and substituting with procedurally generated images shifts feature map statistics across spatial resolutions, miscalibrating anchor boxes and degrading segmentation mask quality on real imagery.
paraphrases.RL: Interactive reinforcement learning is sample-inefficient, and pretraining a policy on synthetic rollouts or scripted trajectories induces reward signal and value function miscalibration that transfers negatively to the real environment.
paraphrases.IR_REC: Training retrieval models requires vast judged pairs, and using synthetic queries/documents in the training set or embedding index miscalibrates query-document scores, lowering recall@k and weakening cross-encoder reranking on real candidate pools.
paraphrases.SPEECH: ASR training needs diverse natural speech, and replacing real recordings with synthetic waveforms or phoneme-level perturbations distorts spectrogram and acoustic frame statistics, causing unstable alignment and higher WER on real utterances.
paraphrases.ROBOTICS: Collecting robot experience is expensive, and substituting real trajectories with simulation-generated data causes a manipulation policy to overfit inaccuracies in the dynamics model, breaking contact reasoning in the sensorimotor loop and limiting sim-to-real transfer.

query_text:
Internal model components correlate with abilities but
correlation is not causation; how can we identify which
components causally implement a given capability and use that
for editing or interpretation?
paraphrase_essence: Correlational associations between internal components and behaviors do not reveal which parts are causally necessary or sufficient for a capability, preventing trustworthy attribution and targeted modification.

paraphrases.LLM_NLP: Capability attribution in large language models is unreliable because correlations between attention head activations and token predictions across decoding steps do not indicate which heads or KV cache entries are causally necessary, so perplexity shifts under ablations are confounded.
paraphrases.MULTIMODAL: In vision-language models, interpretability is confounded because correlations between visual token saliency and outputs (caption quality or VQA accuracy) via cross-modal alignment do not identify which image embeddings are causally responsible for the generated answer.
paraphrases.CV: In dense visual perception, feature attribution fails because correlations between patch token or feature map activations and downstream segmentation mask or anchor box scores do not reveal which receptive fields causally drive predictions.
paraphrases.RL: In reinforcement learning, internal analysis is unreliable because correlations between unit activations and the reward signal across rollouts and trajectories do not establish which parts of the policy or value function are causally responsible for performance.
paraphrases.IR_REC: In information retrieval, model attribution is confounded because correlations between internal features and query-document scores across the candidate pool do not establish which components causally affect reranking and recall@k, making effect predictions under cross-encoder modifications unreliable.
paraphrases.SPEECH: In automatic speech recognition, interpretability is unreliable because correlations between acoustic frame or spectrogram activations and phoneme outputs or alignment do not indicate which components are causally necessary, so WER changes under ablations are unpredictable.
paraphrases.ROBOTICS: In embodied robotics, attribution fails because correlations between internal signals and task success within the sensorimotor loop do not reveal which parts of the manipulation policy or dynamics model causally support the motion plan under sim-to-real shifts.

query_text:
Learned representations are opaque and hard to debug or edit;
how can we reverse-engineer what specific circuits compute,
decompose representations into human-interpretable features,
and verify those decompositions?
paraphrase_essence: Learned models’ internal representations are entangled and opaque, preventing reliable attribution of behavior to human-interpretable components and blocking verification that any proposed decomposition faithfully explains the computation.

paraphrases.LLM_NLP: In large language models, attribution of a decoding step to specific attention heads is unreliable because activations stored in the KV cache are entangled across in-context examples, so changes in perplexity cannot be traced to stable, interpretable subcircuits.
paraphrases.MULTIMODAL: In multimodal models, the image embedding and each visual token are mixed under query-conditioned attention such that cross-modal alignment cannot be localized to identifiable factors, and shifts in VQA accuracy cannot be verified against a consistent attribution.
paraphrases.CV: In dense vision, channel responses in the feature map and patch tokens blend across the receptive field, making it unclear which structures drive predictions, so segmentation mask and anchor box decisions cannot be reliably tied to interpretable components at high spatial resolution.
paraphrases.RL: In reinforcement learning, internal features of the policy and value function are entangled, so action choices within a rollout cannot be attributed to human-interpretable state factors, and variations in the reward signal cannot be verified against a faithful decomposition along the trajectory.
paraphrases.IR_REC: In retrieval systems, dense vectors in the embedding index and cross-encoder scoring are opaque, so the query-document score cannot be decomposed into interpretable facets across the candidate pool, and changes in recall@k cannot be validated against a stable attribution.
paraphrases.SPEECH: In speech models, intermediate representations between the spectrogram and acoustic frames are entangled, so phoneme decisions and alignment cannot be traced to identifiable factors, and fluctuations in WER cannot be verified against a faithful decomposition.
paraphrases.ROBOTICS: In robot control, latent states within the manipulation policy and dynamics model are opaque, so behavior in the sensorimotor loop cannot be attributed to interpretable components, and failures in contact reasoning or a motion plan cannot be verified against a causal decomposition.

query_text:
Models forget previously learned skills when fine-tuned on new
tasks; how can we update a model with new data without
catastrophic forgetting and without retaining all prior data?
paraphrase_essence: Sequential fine-tuning on new tasks causes catastrophic forgetting of prior capabilities due to interference and representation drift when past data cannot be fully retained.

paraphrases.LLM_NLP: During sequential fine-tuning, attention head allocations drift under a new token distribution, and perplexity on prior domains rises when past token sequences are unavailable for revisiting.
paraphrases.MULTIMODAL: In vision–language models, cross-modal alignment degrades and VQA accuracy on earlier datasets drops when image embeddings are updated for a new corpus without access to prior caption–image pairs.
paraphrases.CV: In dense visual perception, updating the backbone on a new distribution shifts feature map statistics and reduces segmentation mask quality on earlier benchmarks when high–spatial-resolution samples from those domains are unavailable.
paraphrases.RL: In continual reinforcement learning, adapting a policy to a new reward signal interferes with the value function and degrades behaviors encoded in prior trajectories when earlier rollouts cannot be accessed.
paraphrases.IR_REC: In retrieval, fine-tuning on fresh logs shifts the embedding index and miscalibrates query–document scores, reducing recall@k on earlier domains when historical candidate pools are not retained for downstream reranking.
paraphrases.SPEECH: In ASR, adapting to a new accent perturbs acoustic frame–phoneme alignment and increases WER on previously supported dialects when prior transcribed audio is unavailable.
paraphrases.ROBOTICS: In continual robot learning, updating a manipulation policy within the sensorimotor loop for a new task perturbs the dynamics model fit and causes previously reliable motion plans to fail when past trajectories are unavailable.

relevance.reasoning: The seed describes a mechanism using lightweight adapter modules (unlearning layers) that selectively modify model behavior on specific data subsets while preserving performance on retained data, using a teacher-student KL divergence objective. The query asks about continual learning / catastrophic forgetting — updating a model with new data without forgetting prior skills, without retaining all prior data. The seed's mechanism (small adapters + selective KL matching on retained vs. modified data) is structurally related to continual learning approaches, but the direction is inverted: the seed is about erasing specific data influence, while the query is about preserving prior knowledge while adding new knowledge. The adapter-based parameter isolation and selective KL-matching on retained data could partially transfer, but the core problem (forgetting during new task learning vs. deliberate unlearning) differs enough that non-trivial adaptation would be needed to repurpose this mechanism for continual learning.

query_text:
Model outputs filtered by a quality signal can serve as new
training data, but this risks reinforcing existing errors; how
can we bootstrap improvement from self-generated data while
avoiding distributional collapse?
paraphrase_essence: When training data are drawn from a model’s own outputs according to a narrow or biased quality signal, feedback loops amplify existing errors and shrink distributional support, causing collapse rather than improvement.

paraphrases.LLM_NLP: In autoregressive LMs, fine-tuning on self-generated tokens that pass a perplexity or logprob filter induces feedback across decoding steps: the token distribution concentrates on a narrow set of continuations, attention heads overfit recurring in-context examples, and generalization degrades due to distributional collapse.
paraphrases.MULTIMODAL: In vision–language learning, reusing model-generated captions or answers accepted by a heuristic scorer skews cross-modal alignment: image embeddings and visual tokens overfit to self-described content, long-tail concepts vanish from supervision, and VQA accuracy drops under open-set queries due to mode collapse.
paraphrases.CV: In dense perception, training on pseudo-labels taken from high-confidence predictions biases the feature map toward large, easy regions: small objects fail to acquire positive anchor boxes, segmentation masks inherit systematic holes, and performance at high spatial resolution collapses on rare classes.
paraphrases.RL: In reinforcement learning, updating the policy primarily from self-generated trajectories filtered by a high reward signal collapses exploration: rollouts concentrate on a narrow behavior manifold, off-distribution errors accumulate, and downstream performance becomes brittle under task variation.
paraphrases.IR_REC: In retrieval with pseudo-relevance feedback, treating top items by the current query-document score as positives narrows the candidate pool distribution: the embedding index drifts toward its own biases, cross-encoder reranking receives too few hard negatives, and recall@k degrades on unseen queries.
paraphrases.SPEECH: In ASR self-training, using decoded transcripts that pass a confidence filter as labels induces alignment drift: acoustic frames for rare phonemes receive little supervision, spectrogram–phoneme timing errors are reinforced, and WER rises on out-of-domain speech.
paraphrases.ROBOTICS: In robot learning from self-collected data, updating a manipulation policy on success-only episodes within the sensorimotor loop narrows coverage: contact reasoning errors compound and sim-to-real robustness degrades due to distributional collapse.

query_text:
Models are brittle to adversarial perturbations imperceptible to
humans; how can pretraining objectives or data augmentation make
models robust without expensive test-time defenses?
paraphrase_essence: Small, human-imperceptible input perturbations cause disproportionate prediction errors, and achieving robustness otherwise depends on expensive test-time defenses.

paraphrases.LLM_NLP: Autoregressive language models fail when tiny, semantically neutral token edits in the prompt trigger large shifts in perplexity across decoding steps; guarding against such perturbations is costly because per-step defenses inflate KV cache traffic and latency.
paraphrases.MULTIMODAL: Vision-language systems fail when imperceptible changes to visual tokens corrupt image embeddings and cross-modal alignment, sharply reducing VQA accuracy under query-conditioned attention; test-time defenses are costly because they add heavy sanitization stages to inference.
paraphrases.CV: Dense visual perception fails when L-infinity-scale pixel noise that is imperceptible to humans perturbs early feature maps, flipping anchor box classifications and corrupting segmentation masks at high spatial resolution; robustness at inference is costly because generic defenses increase compute and latency.
paraphrases.RL: Control policies fail when small adversarial perturbations along the trajectory push the rollout into regions where the value function is miscalibrated, causing large return drops; online robustness is costly because per-rollout safeguards reduce sample efficiency.
paraphrases.IR_REC: Neural retrieval fails when minor, human-preserving text edits move relevant documents outside the embedding index, yielding low recall@k as the candidate pool is adversarially altered; robustness at query time is costly because cross-encoder reranking and broad query-document scoring must be applied.
paraphrases.SPEECH: ASR fails when imperceptible waveform perturbations shift spectrogram statistics and break phoneme alignment, spiking WER; streaming robustness is costly because test-time defenses add buffering and checks that increase streaming latency.
paraphrases.ROBOTICS: Robotic manipulation fails when small observation or actuation perturbations in the sensorimotor loop destabilize the manipulation policy due to brittle contact reasoning in the learned dynamics model; online robustness is costly because frequent motion plan replanning stretches cycle times and resources.
